summary: Dan Shapiro writes an open letter to TechCrunch founder Mike Arrington, asking him to stop investing in startups because it creates conflicts of interest with his journalism. Shapiro argues that economic incentives shape behavior in subtle, unmeasurable ways, and that Arrington's dual role as investor and reporter will inevitably compromise TechCrunch's editorial independence. He frames journalistic independence as a product feature that shouldn't be sacrificed. The letter is notably polite and brief, positioning itself as customer feedback rather than a moral indictment.
key_points: Economic incentives shape behaviors in subtle and unmeasurable ways, even when people try to be fair
Conflicts of interest in journalism don't manifest as obvious bias — they create subtle distortions like over-covering or under-covering portfolio companies
Journalistic independence should be treated as a product feature, not just an ethical principle
The letter frames the argument as pragmatic product feedback rather than moral criticism
money_quotes: I'm a big believer that economic incentives shape behaviors in subtle and unmeasurable ways.
I think your decision to make investments in startups is going to make TechCrunch a worse product.
Are you going to be a little more likely to ignore competitors to your companies? Cover them, even when they're not newsworthy, to show you're not biased?
Feel compelled to pull or throw punches, either to support or prove you're independent of the companies you deal with?
Journalistic independence is a great feature. Please don't cut it.
I'm not making demands or threatening to leave. Just making a request, from one product guy to another.
key_insight: Journalistic independence is best understood not as an abstract ethical ideal but as a core product feature — and conflicts of interest degrade it in ways that are subtle but inevitable.
